key_insight: Solid compact stars can deform about 40% differently from fluid stars, making tidal response a potential probe of whether pulsar-like interiors are solid or fluid.
engineering_relevance: The result links microscopic shear elasticity to observable gravitational-wave and electromagnetic signatures, offering a multi-messenger way to test compact-star material models.
method: Model tidal deformation for solid strangeon stars using a finite shear modulus (bc = 10^34 erg cm^-3).
Compare tidal deformability and I--Love relation deviations against fluid-star predictions for a 1.4 Modot star.
Simulate strain buildup during binary inspiral and estimate fracture energy release near merger.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a 40% difference in tidal deformability, about a 10% I--Love deviation, and up to ~10^46 erg of elastic energy released when strain-triggered fracturing occurs at several hundred Hz.
watch_item: The conclusions depend on the strangeon-star model and assumed shear modulus, so applicability to real pulsar interiors remains uncertain until observational tests confirm solid behavior.
paper_title: Tidal deformation and strain accumulation of solid compact stars
